planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in consistently moist to wet soil, preferably at a streambank, pond margin, or rain garden; Physostegia purpurea spreads by rhizomes and will naturalize into a colony in reliably wet conditions.

directions: Plant in moist to saturated soil at a streambank, pond edge, or rain garden where roots can stay consistently wet; this species is distinct from the more common Physostegia virginiana and thrives in wetter, shadier conditions than its relative. The vivid purple-pink tubular flowers appear in fall and are held on upright spikes; each individual flower can be repositioned laterally on the stem and will stay put, a quirk that earns the plant its common name 'obedient plant.' Divide clumps every two to three years in spring to maintain vigor and prevent the colony from overcrowding neighboring plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil at woodland edges, streambanks, or shaded rain gardens; Stachys tenuifolia spreads by stolons and establishes readily in reliably moist, partially shaded sites.

directions: Stachys tenuifolia is a shade-tolerant native mint-family perennial that forms spreading colonies via stolons in moist woodland edges and floodplain margins where other plants struggle. Small pink-lavender flowers are held on upright spikes in mid-summer and provide nectar for native bees and small pollinators. Plant in reliably moist to wet, organically rich soil and avoid dry or compacted sites; it pairs well with other native wetland-edge plants such as Lobelia cardinalis and Physostegia species. Divide colonies every few years in early spring to keep the planting tidy and share divisions with other moist garden areas.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in permanently moist to wet, organically rich soil at pond margins, swales, or floodplain edges; Lycopus rubellus naturalizes by stolons and is best suited to rain gardens, constructed wetlands, or streambank restoration plantings.

directions: Lycopus rubellus thrives in saturated or inundated soils and is an excellent choice for rain gardens, constructed wetlands, and streambank stabilization plantings where standing water is common in spring. The small white flowers appearing in summer attract native bees and are characteristic of the mint family; the plant is strongly aromatic when the leaves are crushed. Historically, the starchy tubers were eaten by indigenous peoples of the Southeast, and the plant remains an important habitat species supporting wetland food webs. Plant in partial shade with consistently wet roots, and divide every two to three years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow cold stratification over winter for spring germination; Pycnanthemum albescens prefers well-drained to dry, average to poor soil in full to partial sun and is notably drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Pycnanthemum albescens is a coastal plain endemic of the southeastern US with striking silvery-white leaf bracts that surround and contrast dramatically with the small purple flowers in mid- to late summer, creating a two-toned effect visible from a distance. It is among the most valuable native plants for pollinators, drawing extraordinary numbers of native bees, wasps, beneficial flies, and butterflies to its long-blooming flower clusters. Direct sow seeds in fall on the soil surface without covering, as they need light and cold stratification; seedlings transplant poorly so sow in place. Once established, the plant is drought-tolerant and thrives in lean, well-drained soils with little or no supplemental irrigation.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or nursery transplants in spring after all frost danger has passed in extremely well-drained, dry, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Poliomintha longiflora is native to the Chihuahuan Desert and will not tolerate wet or clay soils.

directions: Poliomintha longiflora is a strongly aromatic shrubby herb native to the Chihuahuan Desert and south Texas, widely prized in Mexican regional cuisine as the true 'Mexican oregano' — its flavor is more complex and citrusy than Mediterranean oregano and is considered essential for authentic mole sauces, tamales, and chili con carne. Plant in the hottest, driest, most well-drained spot in the garden; once established it requires little to no supplemental water and thrives on neglect in rocky, alkaline soils. The long, lavender-pink tubular flowers produced from summer through fall are highly attractive to hummingbirds and are among the best late-season nectar sources in xeric gardens. In zone 9 and warmer the plant is evergreen and can be harvested year-round; in zone 7-8 it may die back to the roots in hard winters but re-sprouts reliably in spring.
